details: This package is published as a small, dependency-free ID generator, and its main module is a verbatim copy of a legitimate xid-style library to look benign. Hidden alongside it is a ~290KB file (seed.cjs, with an ESM twin seed.mjs) that is heavily obfuscated (obfuscator[.]io-style: a base64+RC4 string-array decoder, control-flow flattening, and dynamic execution of decoded identifiers) and has nothing to do with ID generation. The package wires up two triggers for this hidden code: a postinstall script (node./dist/cjs/seed.cjs) that executes the payload as soon as the package is installed, and a call in the main module (newState -> runPrepare, run during module initialization) so that merely importing the package also runs it. A one-shot guard flag ensures it executes once. The deobfuscated payload sets up multiple event-stream handlers consistent with a dropper/exfiltration routine. A legitimate ID-generation library has no reason to ship a large obfuscated blob and run it both on install and on import; this is the behaviour of install-time malware disguised as a utility package. @apexcraft/nano-key is a trojanized clone of the legitimate xid-ts ID generation library. The package ships the real library code but adds a postinstall hook (dist/cjs/seed.cjs) and integrates it into the library's initialization path via require('./seed.cjs') and a call to runPrepare(). The seed.cjs file is 292KB of output from the javascript-obfuscator tool, using the dictionary-array and Function-constructor patterns — a common technique to hide the payload's true behaviour from static analysis. The package has no repository and its author identity does not match the repository origin it was cloned from. The obfuscated code executes at install time via the postinstall lifecycle hook, and also when the library's newState() function is called at runtime.
